title: The One Where Nana Dies Twice

expressions.dialogue:
Shelley: **Hey gorgeous, how's it going?**
Chandler: Dehydrated Japanese noodles under fluorescent lights... does it get better than this?
expressions.speaker: Shelley
expressions.meaning: 잘 지내? / 어떻게 지내? — 가장 자연스러운 일상 인사
expressions.nuance: "How are you?"보다 훨씬 캐주얼하고 친근한 표현. 친구나 동료 사이에서 자연스럽게 쓰이며, 진지한 대답보다 "Good, you?" 같은 짧은 응수가 일반적이다.
expressions.example: Hey, long time no see! How's it going? → 야, 오랜만이다! 어떻게 지내?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Hey. Oh, oh, how'd it go?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Hey, how'd it go?

occ.speaker: Susan
occ.text: Hi, how's it goin?

expressions.expression: Does it get better than this? (반어)
expressions.dialogue:
Shelley: Hey gorgeous, how's it going?
Chandler: Dehydrated Japanese noodles under fluorescent lights... **does it get better than this?**
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: (반어) 이보다 더 좋을 수 있겠어? → 현재 상황이 최악이라는 뜻
expressions.nuance: 문자적 의미와 정반대로 쓰이는 반어법. 억양과 맥락이 핵심이며, 지루하거나 불쾌한 상황에서 시니컬하게 쓴다.
expressions.example: Another Monday morning meeting about the meeting... does it get better than this? → 회의에 대한 월요일 아침 회의라니... 이보다 더 좋을 수가 있겠어?

expressions.dialogue:
Shelley: Do you want a date Saturday?
Chandler: Ah, y'see, perfect might be a problem. **Had you said 'co-dependent', or 'self-destructive'...**
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: "If you had said X..."의 도치 표현 — 만약 네가 X라고 했다면
expressions.nuance: "If you had said..."를 도치시킨 다소 격식체 표현. 구어에서도 쓰이며 특히 재치 있게 말을 흐릴 때 유머 효과를 준다.
expressions.example: Had you told me you were vegetarian, I'd have ordered something different. → 네가 채식주의자라고 말했다면 다른 걸 시켰을 텐데.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Janice! There you are! There you are! I had to have one last kiss, and also-also you said that you were going to leave right after I got on the plane!

expressions.dialogue: Ross: No, it's just 'cause, uh, I kinda wanted to **go out with her** too, so I told her, actually, you were seeing Bernie Spellman...
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~와 데이트하다, ~와 사귀다
expressions.nuance: "date someone"보다 훨씬 자연스럽고 흔한 표현. 한 번 나가는 것부터 정식으로 사귀는 것까지 폭넓게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: I finally asked her if she wanted to go out with me. → 드디어 나랑 만나볼 의향이 있냐고 물어봤어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Wait a minute! We're talking about someone that I'm going out with?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on here? You go out with tons of girls.

expressions.dialogue: Shelley: Well yeah! ...Oh God. I- just- I thought- Good, Shelley. **I'm just gonna go flush myself down the toilet now...** (backs out of the room)
expressions.speaker: Shelley
expressions.meaning: 너무 창피해서 사라져버리고 싶다
expressions.nuance: 극도로 당황했을 때 자기 자신을 향해 쓰는 과장된 유머 표현. "I want to disappear"의 더 생생하고 자조적인 버전이다.
expressions.example: I waved back at someone who wasn't waving at me. I wanted to flush myself down the toilet. → 나를 향해 손 흔든 게 아닌데 따라 흔들어버렸어. 진짜 쥐구멍에라도 숨고 싶었어.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: No, it's just 'cause, uh, I **kinda** wanted to go out with her too, so I told her, actually, you were seeing Bernie Spellman...
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: "kind of"의 구어 축약형 — 약간, 어느 정도
expressions.nuance: 직접적인 표현을 부드럽게 만들거나 확신이 100%가 아닐 때 쓴다. 원어민이 매우 자주 쓰는 표현으로, 없으면 어색하게 들릴 수 있다.
expressions.example: I kinda feel like we've met before. → 우리 어디서 만난 것 같은 기분이 들어.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Ooh, I was kinda hoping that wouldn't be an issue...

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Marsha, these are cave people. Okay? They have issues like 'Gee, that glacier's getting kinda close.' See?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Know what was great? The way his smile was kinda crooked.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: ...I kinda wanted to go out with her too, so I told her, actually, you were seeing Bernie Spellman...
Chandler: **You're kidding!** Did you tell her I wasn't?
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 설마! / 진짜? / 농담이지?
expressions.nuance: 믿기 어려운 사실을 들었을 때 내뱉는 반응. "Really?"보다 더 강한 충격이나 어이없음을 담을 수 있다.
expressions.example: She quit her job on the spot? You're kidding! → 그 자리에서 바로 퇴직했다고? 말도 안 돼!

occ.speaker: Frannie
occ.text: Are you kidding? I take credit for Paul. Y'know before me, there was no snap in his turtle for two years.

occ.speaker: All
occ.text: You're kidding. Oh my God.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: No, you kidding? The guy's a freak.. (Ross enters off camera)

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Well, this is fascinating. So, uh, **what is it about me?**
Phoebe: I dunno, 'cause you're smart, you're funny...
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 내 어떤 점 때문에 그런 거야? / 나의 뭐가 그런 인상을 주는 거야?
expressions.nuance: 타인이 자신에 대해 특정한 인상을 갖는 이유를 물을 때 쓰는 자연스러운 표현. "Why do people think that about me?"보다 훨씬 구어적이다.
expressions.example: What is it about me that makes people think I'm unapproachable? → 내 어떤 점 때문에 사람들이 나를 다가가기 어렵다고 느끼는 걸까?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What is it? Is there something fundamentally unmarriable about me?

occ.speaker: Chan
occ.text: OK, what is it about me? Do I not look fun enough? Is there something. . . repellant. . . about me?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What is it? Is there something fundamentally unmarriable about me?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Okay, I-I d'know, **you-you just- you have a quality.**
All: Yes. Absolutely. A quality.
Chandler: Oh, oh, a quality, good, because I was worried you guys were gonna be vague about this.
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 딱 꼬집어 말하기 어렵지만 뭔가 특별한 느낌/분위기가 있다
expressions.nuance: 구체적으로 설명하기 어려울 때 쓰는 모호하지만 의미 있는 표현. 이 에피소드에서는 개그 포인트지만 실제로도 "there's something about you" 처럼 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: I can't explain it, but she has a quality that makes everyone feel at ease. → 설명하기 어렵지만, 그녀에게는 모두를 편안하게 만드는 뭔가가 있어.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Twice.
Joey: Twice?
Phoebe: **Oh, that sucks!**
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 그거 정말 안됐다 / 최악이다 / 너무 별로다
expressions.nuance: 상대방의 불운에 공감하거나 나쁜 상황을 표현할 때 쓰는 매우 일상적인 표현. 격식 있는 자리에서는 피하는 게 좋다.
expressions.example: You had to work on your birthday? That sucks! → 생일에 일해야 했다고? 진짜 최악이다!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: So, the ebola virus. That's gotta suck, huh?

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Listen, sorry about your death, that really sucks.

occ.speaker: Gunther
occ.text: That sucks. I was buried in an avalanche.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: I think once you're dead, you're dead! You're gone! **You're worm food!** (realises his tactlessness) ...So Chandler looks gay, huh?
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 죽어서 땅속에서 벌레의 먹이가 된다 → 완전히 끝난 것
expressions.nuance: 죽음을 매우 직설적이고 거칠게 표현하는 속어. 슬프거나 공식적인 자리에서는 절대 쓰지 않는다. Joey의 눈치 없는 솔직함을 보여주는 표현이다.
expressions.example: He's a total pessimist — he thinks when you're gone, you're just worm food and that's it. → 그는 완전 허무주의자야 — 죽으면 그냥 땅속 벌레 밥이 되는 거라고 생각해.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: Unless you want your mother to spend eternity in a lemon yellow pant-suit, **go with the burgundy.**
Aunt Lillian: You know, whatever we pick, she would've told us it's the wrong one.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~로 하다, ~을 선택하다
expressions.nuance: 여러 선택지 중 하나를 고를 때 "choose" 대신 쓰이는 자연스러운 구어 표현. 추천할 때는 "I'd go with..."로 많이 쓴다.
expressions.example: If you're not sure which option to pick, just go with your gut. → 어떤 걸 선택할지 모르겠으면 그냥 직감대로 해.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Anyway, they want me to go down to this- sonogram thing with them tomorrow.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yes, 'cause now I have to go down there, and deal with them.

expressions.dialogue:
Mr. Geller: C'mon, **you'll make a day of it!** You'll rent a boat, pack a lunch...
Monica: ...And then we throw your body in the water... Gee, that does sound fun.
expressions.speaker: Mr. Geller
expressions.meaning: 어떤 활동을 하루 종일 즐기는 나들이로 만들다
expressions.nuance: 보통 의무적이거나 평범한 일을 빌미로 하루를 즐겁게 보내자고 제안할 때 쓴다. 긍정적이고 활기찬 뉘앙스다.
expressions.example: Since we have to go to IKEA anyway, let's make a day of it — brunch first, then shopping. → 어차피 이케아 가야 하니까 하루 코스로 만들자 — 브런치 먹고 쇼핑하자.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: You and Milton have to join us on the boat. Karen'll pack a lunch, you'll bring the kids, we'll make a day of it.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: Excuse me? You don't think I could get a Brian?
Shelley: Well, I think **Brian's a little out of your league.**
expressions.speaker: Shelley
expressions.meaning: 상대가 나에게는 너무 수준이 높다 / 어울리기엔 너무 훌륭하다
expressions.nuance: 주로 연애 맥락에서 상대가 자신보다 훨씬 매력적이거나 뛰어날 때 쓴다. 냉정하게 말하거나 자조적으로도 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: I know she's out of my league, but I'm still going to ask her out. → 나한테 과분한 사람인 거 알지만, 그래도 고백해볼 거야.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh please, could she be more out of my league? Ross, back me up here.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Now, you do realize that she's a cartoon, and way out of your league?

occ.speaker: Guru Saj
occ.text: (entering) Hello, I am Guru Saj-(sees the duck)-Whoa!! (to Joey) That's supposed to be a duck right? 'Cause otherwise, this is waaay out of my league.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: I could get a Brian. **Believe you me.** ...I'm really not.
Shelley: Is Brian...?
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: "Believe me"의 강조형 — 진짜야, 믿어봐
expressions.nuance: "you"가 목적어이지만 앞으로 나온 고어적 어순. 오늘날엔 약간 과장되거나 유머러스한 뉘앙스로 쓰이며, 자신의 주장을 강하게 밀어붙일 때 효과적이다.
expressions.example: This shortcut will save us at least an hour, believe you me. → 이 지름길이 한 시간은 아껴줄 거야, 진짜로.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Pheebs, I can't believe he hasn't kissed you yet. I mean God, by my sixth date with Paolo, I mean he had already named both my breasts! ...Ooh. Did I just share too much?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: (to Ross) I can't believe you don't want to know. I mean, I couldn't not know, I mean, if, if the doctor knows, and Carol knows, and Susan knows....

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I just can't believe you're in here. I mean, what am I gonna do without you? Who's gonna crash the embassy parties with me? Who's gonna take me to the Big Apple Circus?

expressions.dialogue:
Mrs. Geller: **What's with your hair?**
Monica: What?
Mrs. Geller: What's different?
Monica: Nothing.
expressions.speaker: Mrs. Geller
expressions.meaning: 네 X가 왜 그래? / X가 어떻게 된 거야?
expressions.nuance: 뭔가 평소와 다르거나 이상해 보일 때 쓰는 표현. 비판적이거나 단순히 궁금한 뉘앙스 모두 가능하며 "What happened to your X?"보다 더 일상적이다.
expressions.example: What's with your voice today? Are you getting sick? → 오늘 목소리가 왜 그래? 감기 걸렸어?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: See ya.... Waitwait, what's with you?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (trying desperately to change the subject) So, Ross, what's going on with you? Any stories? (Digs her elbow into his hand.) No news, no little anecdotes to share with the folks?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on with you?

expressions.expression: a matter of hours / minutes / days
expressions.dialogue:
Aunt Lillian: The doctor says **it's a matter of hours.**
Monica: How-how are you, Mom?
expressions.speaker: Aunt Lillian
expressions.meaning: 불과 몇 시간/분/일의 문제 — 곧 일어날 일
expressions.nuance: "a matter of + 시간 단위"는 긴박감이나 임박함을 표현할 때 쓴다. "only a matter of time"처럼 피할 수 없는 일에도 확장해 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: The results should be back soon — it's just a matter of hours. → 결과가 곧 나올 거야 — 몇 시간 안에.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: You don't get a lot of 'doy' these days...

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: "Warden, in five minutes my pain will be over. But you'll have to live with the knowledge that you sent an honest man to die."

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Eleven days before Halloween.. all the good costumes are gone?

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: So- you can tell?
Lowell: **Pretty much,** most of the time. We have a kind of... radar.
expressions.speaker: Lowell
expressions.meaning: 거의, 대체로, 어느 정도는 그래
expressions.nuance: "almost" 또는 "basically yes"의 캐주얼 버전. 100% 확신하지 않지만 대략 맞다고 할 때 쓰인다. 원어민이 매우 자주 쓰는 표현이다.
expressions.example: Are you done with the report? Pretty much — just need to proofread it. → 보고서 다 끝났어? 거의 — 검토만 하면 돼.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Yeah, I think for us, kissing is pretty much like an opening act, y'know? I mean it's like the stand-up comedian you have to sit through before Pink Floyd comes out.

occ.speaker: Pizza Guy
occ.text: No, pretty much just a towel.

occ.speaker: Bob
occ.text: Yep. Pretty much.

expressions.expression: some things are better left unsaid
expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Do you think things would have been better if you'd just told her the truth?
Mrs. Geller: ...No. I think **some things are better left unsaid.** I think it's nicer when people just get along.
expressions.speaker: Mrs. Geller
expressions.meaning: 어떤 말들은 안 하는 게 더 낫다
expressions.nuance: 갈등을 피하거나 관계를 지키기 위해 솔직함보다 침묵을 택할 때 쓰는 표현. 지혜처럼 들리지만 회피의 뉘앙스도 함께 담겨 있다.
expressions.example: She asked me what I thought of her new haircut. Some things are better left unsaid. → 새 머리 어떠냐고 물어봤어. 어떤 말은 안 하는 게 나아.

expressions.dialogue:
Mrs. Geller: Do you know what it's like to grow up with someone who is critical of every single thing you say?
Monica: **...I can imagine.**
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: (문자적) 상상할 수 있어 → (반어) 너무 잘 알지, 직접 당하고 있거든
expressions.nuance: 직접 경험한 사람이 "상상한다"고 말하는 것 자체가 아이러니. 말은 동의처럼 들리지만 그 안에 불만이나 비판이 숨어 있는 절제된 반응이다.
expressions.example: Working under a micromanager must be exhausting." "I can imagine." (said by someone who has one) → "마이크로매니저 밑에서 일하면 지치겠다." "충분히 상상이 가네." (직접 당하는 사람이 할 때)

occ.speaker: Ticket Agent
occ.text: I can't imagine why.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Well look, I'm breaking up with Katie so I had to put on some extra padding. Y'know? I mean, if she hits me when she's happy, can you imagine how hard she's gonna hit me when I tell her I'm taking away the Joey love?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (starts to imagine it) I can't. I keep seeing it the good way.

expressions.dialogue:
Shelley: Y'know, **it's a shame,** because you and Lowell would've made a great couple.
Chandler: Lowell? Financial Services' Lowell, that's who you saw me with?
expressions.speaker: Shelley
expressions.meaning: 아쉽다, 안타깝다
expressions.nuance: 어떤 상황이 잘 되지 않아 아쉬울 때 쓰는 표현. "That's too bad"와 비슷하지만 조금 더 진심 어린 안타까움을 담는다.
expressions.example: It's a shame you can't make it to the dinner — everyone's been asking about you. → 저녁 못 온다니 아쉽다 — 다들 네 얘기를 물어봤는데.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: It's such a shame you can't see which finger I'm holding up.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh, well, I guess I had that one coming. I'm just gonna throw it out, it's probably just a bunch of shampoo and... (she opens the box and stops)

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, it's a real shame you can't make it to that one-woman show tonight.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: **Check it out,** Giants-Cowboys. (He has a pocket TV)
Chandler: You're watching a football game at a funeral?
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 이것 좀 봐 / 이거 봐봐
expressions.nuance: 뭔가 흥미롭거나 신기한 것을 상대방에게 보여주거나 알릴 때 쓰는 캐주얼한 표현. "Look at this!"와 같은 뜻이지만 더 흥분되고 구어적이다.
expressions.example: Check it out — they finally opened that ramen place we've been waiting for! → 이것 좀 봐 — 우리가 기다리던 라멘 가게 드디어 열었어!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: (to Ross) Hey! Here's the birthday boy! Ross, check it out: hockey tickets, Rangers-Penguins, tonight at the Garden, and we're taking you.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Check it out, check it out. Heckles' high school yearbook.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Hey, hey, check it out, guess what I got.

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: Oh, you-you mean your earrings?
Phoebe: **What'd I say?**
Rachel: (sticking her foot out) Hm-m.
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 내가 뭐라고 했어? (자기 실수를 전혀 인지하지 못할 때)
expressions.nuance: "What did I say?"의 축약형. 자신이 잘못 말했는데 전혀 모르고 순진하게 되묻는 상황에서 쓰인다. 인지하지 못한 실수에 대한 진짜 혼란을 표현한다.
expressions.example: You said 'Tuesday,' not 'Thursday.'" "Wait, what'd I say? → '화요일'이라고 했잖아, '목요일'이 아니라." "잠깐, 내가 뭐라고 했다고?

expressions.expression: I'm not sure what you're getting at
expressions.dialogue:
Monica: Do you think things would have been better if you'd just told her the truth?
Mrs. Geller: **I'm not sure I know what you're getting at.**
expressions.speaker: Mrs. Geller
expressions.meaning: 네가 무슨 말을 하려는 건지 모르겠어 / 핵심이 뭔데?
expressions.nuance: 상대가 돌려 말하거나 암시할 때 요점을 직접 물을 때 쓴다. "What are you trying to say?"보다 조금 더 정중한 표현이다.
expressions.example: We've all been working really hard lately, don't you think?" "I'm not sure what you're getting at. → 우리 다 요즘 진짜 열심히 일하고 있잖아, 그렇지?" "무슨 말을 하려는 건지 모르겠는데.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: The point is, if you were gonna set me up with someone, **I'd like to think** you'd set me up with someone like him.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 나는 ~이길 바란다 / 그래야 한다고 생각하고 싶다
expressions.nuance: 기대나 희망을 자신감 있게 표현할 때 쓰는 표현. "I hope"보다 약간 더 자기주장이 담겨 있으며, 자신의 가치나 기준을 부드럽게 강조할 때 유용하다.
expressions.example: I'd like to think I've been a fair and supportive manager to everyone on the team. → 나는 팀원 모두에게 공정하고 지지적인 매니저였다고 생각하고 싶어.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Anyway um, when he left he forgot to take the poem with him. Now, I am like totally dense about poetry, but I think it's pretty good all right. Check it out. (hands them the poem)

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Hey, Frank. Look, okay, I know that you think I did like this totally evil thing, but I so didn't. There's someone here who can explain this better than I can.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Well, I'd like to think there was something for everyone. Look, I know you're casting for this new show...
